tam.subtitle: Singapore corporate training-automation spend at the LMS-platform + content-tooling layer — excluding government-funded delivery channels and university-credentialing.

stacks.name: SG mid-market employers (30–500 HC) — LMS + content tools
stacks.source: ACRA / SSIC corporate registry + DOS Business Counts Q4 2025 (~7,400 SG firms in 30–500 HC band, services-led).

stacks.name: SG large employers (500–2,000 HC) — sub-enterprise LMS spend
stacks.source: DOS Business Counts; firms below Cornerstone/Workday/SAP procurement thresholds (~900 SG firms in band).

stacks.name: SG training providers + EA-agencies + multi-entity SMBs — micro-tenancy
stacks.source: MOM EA-licence registry (~1,200 EA-agencies) + WSG-listed training providers (~700) + multi-entity SMB consolidators.

sam.subtitle: TAM filtered to firms with browser-based white-collar workforces, existing deck-heavy training material, and willingness to buy SaaS without RFP.
filters: ≥30 HC employer (small-team carve-out below this — they'll stay DIY)
Browser-based workforce (excludes deskless/blue-collar — out of scope by brief)
SaaS-procurement-ready (no 12-month RFP; can swipe card up to S$5K/mo)
PDPA-sensitive enough to prefer SG-region hosting (filters out fully Workday/SAP-locked estates)

stacks.name: Mid-market services (30–500 HC) with deck-led training
stacks.source: SG services sector ~55% of mid-market firms (DOS Services Sector Survey 2024); ~60% of those have deck-led training (industry interview triangulation).

stacks.name: Sub-enterprise (500–2,000 HC) NOT locked into Workday/SAP/Cornerstone
stacks.source: Workday/SAP SuccessFactors/Cornerstone combined SG penetration at this band ~40% (vendor sales calls + Gartner SG note 2024).

stacks.name: EA-agencies + training providers + multi-entity SMB tenancy
stacks.source: EA-agency segment self-identified as primary affinity wedge (recruiter/BD content overlap with Elitez library); white-label appeal for HRMS/EOR partners.

som.subtitle: Realistic 3-year revenue capture under direct outbound + partner channel (HRMS/EOR + EA-agency networks); steady-state Y3 ≈ 7% of SAM.
som.total_equation: Y1 0.2 + Y2 0.6 + Y3 1.1 (cumulative annualised at Y3 exit)

stacks.name: Year 1 — direct outbound, EA-agency seed
stacks.source: Conservative 60 paying customers Y1 (avg 25 paid seats × S$2.99 + ~30% on Pro at S$99 platform fee). Internal sales model.

stacks.name: Year 2 — HRMS/EOR partner channel opens (white-label + bundle)
stacks.source: Two HRMS partners + one EOR partner; channel-led 200 customers; uplift from Pro→Scale on co-branded deals.

stacks.name: Year 3 — multi-entity SMB consolidators + Enterprise white-label
stacks.source: ~300 customers across direct + channel; meaningful Enterprise ($299 platform) anchors; net dollar retention ~115%.

implications.headline: Sub-S$70M TAM means no land-grab — every dollar earned, not subsidised
implications.body: With grants stripped from the pitch, addressable SG spend is ~S$62M. A 5–10% steady-state share is a profitable PE-style SaaS, not a venture rocket. Plan a 24–36 month bootstrapped path to S$1.5–2M ARR; the model must work without raising.

implications.headline: The S$99–S$299 platform band is structurally vacant for AI-ingest LMS
implications.body: Cornerstone/Workday/SAP gate below 500 seats; Coursebox/Synthesia price per-seat without a platform-flat anchor; HRMS-bundled learn modules are checklist-shaped not authoring. Our Pro/Scale/Enterprise sit exactly where mid-market wants to buy. Defend this band aggressively.

implications.headline: EA-agencies and multi-entity SMB consolidators are the high-velocity wedge
implications.body: ~1,200 SG EA-agencies + ~700 training providers + multi-entity SMBs (one buyer, many tenants) account for ~S$6M of SAM but offer the fastest sales cycles because the Elitez library hits 80% of their use-case on day one. Open with this wedge, expand to generic mid-market by Y2.

implications.headline: DIY (SharePoint/Forms, Google Forms, Notion) is the real incumbent — not Articulate
implications.body: Most sub-200-HC SG firms have never bought an LMS. The competitive frame must be 'replace your shared-drive chaos with auditable training' more than 'switch from Articulate'. Sales collateral should lead with manager time saved + audit trail, not authoring-tool comparison tables.

market_size.methodology_appendix: TAM is built from three layers of SG corporate training-automation spend explicitly excluding (a) government-funded delivery channels (SkillsFuture-funded courses, WSG-funded programmes — intentionally off our marketing pitch per the recalibrated brief), (b) university and polytechnic credentialing, (c) MNC global procurement that flows through Workday/SAP/Cornerstone at scale, and (d) deskless/blue-collar segments (out-of-scope per brief). Layer one: SG mid-market firms (30–500 HC), ~7,400 entities per DOS Business Counts Q4 2025 cross-referenced against ACRA SSIC services-sector filings. We assume an average S$3K–S$8K annual LMS + content-tooling spend; the midpoint of S$4K reflects that many of these firms currently spend zero on a dedicated LMS but do spend on M365/Workspace seats, generic compliance e-learning subscriptions, and ad-hoc training-provider engagements that aggregate to this band. Layer two: SG sub-enterprise (500–2,000 HC), ~900 firms below the typical Cornerstone/Workday/SAP procurement threshold, where annualised LMS spend lands in the S$15K–S$25K band (above SMB SaaS but below enterprise). Layer three: SG training providers, EA-licensed recruitment agencies, and multi-entity SMB consolidators — ~2,800 buyers who fit a micro-tenancy or multi-tenant procurement model at S$3K–S$8K each. TAM totals ~S$62M, conservatively rounded. SAM applies five filters: ≥30 HC employer, browser-based white-collar workforce (the brief's explicit fence — no deskless/blue-collar), existing deck-heavy training material, SaaS-procurement-ready (sub-S$5K/mo wallet without RFP), and PDPA-sensitivity sufficient to prefer SG-region hosting. The filtered share of TAM lands at ~44%, yielding ~S$27M SAM. SOM models a 3-year bootstrapped capture path: Y1 direct + EA-agency seed (~60 customers, ~S$200K ARR), Y2 HRMS/EOR partner channel opens (~200 customers, ~S$770K ARR), Y3 multi-entity SMB consolidators + Enterprise white-label anchors (~300 customers, ~S$1.1M ARR exit run-rate). Cumulative-revenue-during-period framing puts SOM at ~S$1.9M. This is intentionally conservative — at ~7% of SAM steady-state Y3, it presumes no venture-funded acceleration. The earlier S$1.08B+ inflation built on SkillsFuture's annual training spend is rejected here because (a) that pool funds delivery channels not LMS platforms, and (b) the recalibrated brief explicitly drops grant-funded distribution from the marketing pitch. Sources: DOS Business Counts (Q4 2025), ACRA SSIC corporate registry, MOM EA-licence registry, WSG SkillsFuture training-provider directory (used for buyer count only, not as a TAM driver), vendor self-disclosed SG penetration in sales calls, Gartner SG HCM note 2024 (Cornerstone/Workday/SAP penetration at sub-enterprise band).

sources.title: ACRA SSIC corporate registry (services sector filings)
WSG SkillsFuture training-provider directory (buyer-count reference only)
Talenox public pricing — SG HRMS reference
IMDA AI Verify Foundation (governance framework for SG AI deployments)
PDPC PDPA guidelines (Personal Data Protection Commission SG)

policies.title: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) — SG-region data-residency expectations
policies.body: PDPC's 2024 model AI governance refresh and revised data-portability guidance increase SG buyers' preference for in-region hosting of HR + training data, particularly when employee-identifying records (course history, quiz scores, manager dashboards) accrue inside the LMS. Offshore-hosted LMS now triggers transfer-limitation diligence on every procurement.
policies.implication_for_us: SG-region hosting is a procurement-clearing argument vs Coursebox (AU/US), Articulate (US), Docebo (CA), TalentLMS (US), Disprz (IN). Make residency a first-screen feature, not a footnote.

policies.title: IMDA AI Verify Foundation — governance framework for SG AI deployments
policies.body: AI Verify (open-sourced 2023, foundation launched 2024) gives SG buyers a structured testing toolkit for AI fairness, transparency, and robustness. Increasingly cited in mid-market RFPs as a baseline expectation when AI-generated content is delivered to employees.
policies.implication_for_us: Position the AI quiz-generation and reverse-engineering pipeline as AI Verify-aligned: human-in-the-loop review on first publish, source-document traceability per quiz item, audit log of AI edits.

policies.title: MOM TAFEP Fair Consideration Framework — role-based content scoping
policies.body: Tripartite Alliance for Fair & Progressive Employment Practices guidance on fair recruitment, role-based training, and non-discrimination in promotion. Mid-market employers increasingly fold TAFEP into onboarding curricula to demonstrate compliance posture.
policies.implication_for_us: Role-based learning tracks (Scale tier) map directly onto TAFEP fair-consideration framing. The Elitez library's TAFEP module is a credible compliance-ready starting point — keep it prominent in the SG-baseline content pitch.

policies.title: IHRP HR-data PDPA guidance — HR-specific data-handling baseline
policies.body: Institute for Human Resource Professionals (IHRP) HR-data PDPA refresh sets HR-specific expectations on retention, access, and breach response for employee training records. Increasingly used as a procurement sanity-check by SG HR Managers.
policies.implication_for_us: Reference IHRP guidance in security/privacy collateral — it's a HR-Manager-trusted source. Particularly useful when displacing SharePoint/Forms which have no HR-tailored access-control story.

policies.title: Singapore Companies Act + ACRA audit-trail expectations
policies.body: Companies Act requirements for record-keeping (training completion as evidence of duty-of-care for regulated industries — finance, healthcare, security services) push buyers toward systems with cryptographic-grade audit logs.
policies.implication_for_us: Audit-log capability (Enterprise tier) is the structural argument against DIY SharePoint/Forms — a M365 Forms response export is not the same as a tamper-evident training-completion ledger. Bring this front-of-pitch for regulated-vertical buyers.

policies.title: IRAS IR8A / IR21 documentation hygiene (payroll-adjacent training context)
policies.body: IRAS annual IR8A filings and IR21 cessation notices require employer record-keeping around employee status changes; well-trained HR Ops staff reduce errors. Training content for HR Ops modules ties to these workflows.
policies.implication_for_us: Keep IR8A/IR21 modules in the HR Ops role track but don't lead the marketing pitch with them — they're library-baseline credibility, not the wedge.

cultural_signals.observation: SG SME owners and Ops Directors describe training as 'the deck I wrote that nobody reads' rather than 'the course we built'.
cultural_signals.evidence: Cross-validated in informal interviews 2025–2026 with EA-agency principals, professional-services partners, and SG mid-market HR managers; consistently the deck-in-shared-drive complaint dominates over authoring-tool complaint.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Frame the product as 'turn your deck into training' rather than 'build courses' — the mental model is reuse, not authoring. Marketing site, sales scripts, and onboarding flow must all speak deck-language.

cultural_signals.observation: Post-2025 GenAI rollouts have created a new mandatory training surface — AI use policy, prompt craft, AI risks — that almost no SG SME has documented.
cultural_signals.evidence: IMDA Model AI Governance Framework v2 (2024); PDPC Advisory Guidelines on Use of Personal Data in AI Systems (2024); rising RFP mentions of 'AI use policy training' in mid-market HR procurement.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: The AI-literacy modules in the Elitez baseline (mod-genai, mod-llm-basics, mod-prompt-craft, mod-ai-risks, mod-ai-policy) are a near-monopoly position in SG. Lead with this in 2026 sales — buyers are urgently asked for an answer and none of the big LMS vendors ship SG-localised AI policy content.

cultural_signals.observation: SG buyers default-distrust offshore-hosted HR/training data after multiple cross-border incidents in 2023–2025.
cultural_signals.evidence: PDPC enforcement decisions 2023–2025; broader rise of 'data-residency clauses' in SG mid-market procurement templates.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Residency is a procurement-clearing argument, not a nice-to-have. Build a one-pager: 'SG-region hosting / SG-incorporated entity / PDPA-aligned controls' — and surface it in the first pricing conversation.

cultural_signals.observation: Mid-market SG managers describe spending 5–15 hours per quarter answering the same training questions in 1:1 because the deck is in a shared drive nobody reads.
cultural_signals.evidence: Brief (audience_signals) + EA-agency principal interviews 2025–2026; range varies by team size but the complaint is universal.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Manager-time-saved is a more credible monetisable claim than 'employee skills uplift'. Make the ROI story arithmetic-grounded: 1 day of HR Manager time at S$4–600 vs S$99 platform + ~S$2.99/seat.

cultural_signals.observation: SG SMEs avoid annual lock-in for SaaS under S$1K/mo — month-to-month is the assumed default.
cultural_signals.evidence: SG SaaS pricing surveys (vendor self-disclosure 2024–25); explicit 'no annual lock-in' framing wins on most SG-targeted SaaS pricing pages under S$500/mo.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Keep month-to-month positioning on Starter/Team/Pro tiers. Negotiate annual lock-ins privately on Scale + Enterprise where the discount mechanic is more typical.

economic_signals.indicator: SG SME ICT-on-software spend YoY (2024 vs 2023)
economic_signals.value: +6.2% YoY (S$2.1B → S$2.23B; SMEs only, excludes MNC SG-entity spend)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Underlying budget growth supports SaaS expansion. Sub-S$1K/mo bands of SME software spend grew faster than enterprise; our $99–$299 platform-flat sits in the growing band.

economic_signals.indicator: SG mid-market wage inflation (HR Manager loaded rate)
economic_signals.value: Loaded hourly ~S$60–S$90/hr 2026 (MOM Occupational Wage Survey 2024 + IHRP benchmark uplift)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Manager-time-saved arithmetic now anchors at S$500–S$700 for one day of HR Manager time per quarter. Use this in pricing collateral as the NBA baseline for the 100–500 HC buyer.

economic_signals.indicator: SG SaaS willingness-to-pay sub-S$10/seat band (HRMS reference: Talenox S$5; JustLogin S$3–6)
economic_signals.value: S$3–S$10/user/mo modal range for SG SMB-bundled HR tooling
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Our S$2.99/seat sits below the modal HRMS bundle band — psychologically safe for the SME buyer to swipe a card. The platform-flat fee is where capability differentiation must be priced, not the per-seat.

economic_signals.indicator: SG EOR market growth (Multiplier, Deel, Velocity Global, Remote)
economic_signals.value: Triple-digit growth across SG-headcount EOR customer counts 2022–25 (vendor disclosures + Tech in Asia coverage)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: EOR partners are the highest-leverage channel partner for the Enterprise tier (white-label learning bundled into EOR onboarding). Prioritise outbound to Multiplier (SG-HQ) and Deel SG.

adoption_patterns.note: SG SMEs (≤200 HC) with any dedicated LMS ~18% — most use DIY SharePoint/Forms or Google Workspace + ad-hoc training-provider engagements. SG MNCs ~72% have some LMS, typically Workday/SAP/Cornerstone via global procurement. Our wedge is the 82% of SG SMEs without a dedicated LMS, plus the sub-enterprise band where MNC procurement hasn't landed.

trends.title: AI-authoring shifts from prompt-driven to source-driven (BYO-content) — the wedge isn't AI, it's WHERE the AI starts
trends.evidence: Coursebox AI (doc-to-course, 2023–25 growth), Docebo Shape (source-to-course, 2024 GA), Disprz Author AI (source ingest 2024), Articulate AI Assistant (Jan 2025) — note Articulate is text-rewrite assist, NOT source-ingest. The category is bifurcating into prompt-driven (TalentLMS TalentCraft, LearnWorlds, Tovuti) and source-driven (Coursebox, Docebo Shape, Disprz, us).
trends.implication_for_us: Frame the wedge as 'AI ingests YOUR content' not 'AI authoring'. Sales decks should show the source PPT → modular output transformation, not generic AI-generated text. Differentiation collapses if we sound like Coursebox; differentiation holds if we ground in the SG library + reverse-engineering claim.

trends.title: HRMS-bundled learning is squeezing standalone LMS at the SME entry tier
trends.evidence: Talenox roadmap (Learn module 2025), JustLogin Learning add-on, BambooHR Learning, Deel Engage (Jan 2024 launch). The pitch is 'one bill, one login' to the buyer already inside the HRMS wallet.
trends.implication_for_us: Defend the S$99–S$299 platform band — that's where the HRMS bundle stops being competitive. HRMS Learn modules are checklist-shaped, not authoring. Build white-label as a channel weapon: partner with HRMS providers so we ARE their Learn module on the higher tier.

trends.title: Per-question analytics + retry heatmaps are emerging as the real DIY-displacement argument
trends.evidence: Quiz-level analytics now table-stakes in Tovuti, 360Learning, Docebo; emerging in TalentLMS. SharePoint/Forms remain quiz-level only (% correct), not per-question. SG mid-market HR Managers increasingly cite 'I want to know WHICH questions people fail' as a buying criterion.
trends.implication_for_us: Per-question + per-module heatmaps (Scale tier) is the wedge against DIY. Lead the Scale-tier pitch with knowledge-gap heatmaps; this is the only feature DIY genuinely cannot match.

trends.title: AI-policy and AI-use training is becoming a 2026 mandatory module for SG mid-market
trends.evidence: IMDA Model AI Governance v2 (2024); PDPC AI advisory (2024); rising RFP mentions in SG mid-market HR procurement; corporate D&O insurance carriers asking for AI-use-policy attestation in 2026 renewals.
trends.implication_for_us: Lead 2026 sales with the AI literacy + AI policy modules. The Elitez baseline has 4–5 modules covering this; no global LMS ships SG-localised AI policy content. Highest-leverage near-monopoly play.

trends.title: White-label + multi-tenant is becoming a channel-partner expectation, not a tier upsell
trends.evidence: EOR providers (Deel, Multiplier, Remote) increasingly bundle learning into their stack; HRMS providers (Talenox, JustLogin) looking for white-label content partners to fill out their stack; consultancies want their brand on training they sell to clients.
trends.implication_for_us: White-label (Enterprise tier) is a wedge into the EOR + HRMS channel. Build a clean partner-API + sub-tenancy architecture early; this is the highest-LTV customer shape we can land.

trends.title: Voice/avatar AI video is fragmenting authoring rather than consolidating it
trends.evidence: Synthesia, HeyGen, ElevenLabs growth 2024–25; most enterprise buyers pair these with separate LMSs rather than buying a single avatar-LMS.
trends.implication_for_us: Don't compete with Synthesia/HeyGen on video generation — integrate. A clean 'embed Synthesia/HeyGen video into a module' workflow is more valuable than an in-house video tool. Roadmap item for Scale+, not table-stakes.
